title: Enumerated type
content:
Enumerated type # An enumerated type is a finite set of alternative values. This is a more readable alternative to so-called “magic numbers”.
Example. A traffic light can have three colors: green, yellow and red.
We could model this with a “magic number”. For instance, a variable x with type integer, where:
x < 0 means green, x == 0 means yellow, x > 0 means red. Instead, we can declare an enumerated type (for instance named Light) with 3 possible values (Green, Yellow and Red), and use a variable of this type. Then instead of
x < 0,
we can write
x == Red,
which is more readable.
In Haskell # Syntax. An enumerated type in Haskell can be written
data <enumerated type name> = <first value > | ... | <last value> where:
the type name and the values start with a capital letter, and values are distinct. Example.
data Light = Green | Yellow | Red Note. The type Bool in Haskell is an enumerated type (with values True and False).
An enumerated type can be used like a regular one (e.g. in a pattern or a Boolean condition).
Example.
data Light = Green | Yellow | Red -- pattern matching canPass :: Light -> Bool canPass Red = False canPass _ = True -- Boolean condition isRisky :: Int -> Light -> Bool isRisky speed state = speed > 30 && state != Green

title: Fold
content:
Fold # A variety of operations on a list can be understood as combining its values inductively, either from left to right or from right to left.
Example. Consider the list
$\qquad 1, 2, 3, 4$
The sum of its elements can be computed inductively as:
$((1 + 2) + 3) + 4\qquad$ (left-to-right), or $1 + (2 + (3 + 4))\qquad$ (right-to-left) Similarly, the least element in this list can be computed as
$\min(\min(\min(1, 2), 3), 4)\qquad$ (left-to-right), or $\min(1, \min(2, \min(3, 4)))\qquad$ (right-to-left) Note. At first sight, these expressions may look different for + and $\min$. The only reason is that + is traditionally written in infix form, whereas $\min$ is more often written in prefix form. To convince yourself that these expressions have the same structure, you may for instance replace each $(a + b)$ in the first expression with $\mathsf{sum} (a,b)$.
Generalization. This naturally leads to defining two higher-order functions (left-to-right and right-to-left) that take as argument a binary operation $\oplus$ (e.g. + or $\min$), and apply it inductively to a list:
$((1 \oplus 2) \oplus 3) \oplus 4\qquad$ (left-to-right), or $1 \oplus (2 \oplus (3 \oplus 4))\qquad$ (right-to-left) Warning. $+$ is an associative operator, meaning that
$\qquad(a + b) + c = b + (a + c)$
And similarly for $\min$.
For these operators, left-to-right and right-to-left evaluation coincide. In contrast, for a non-associative operation (like $-$, $/$, set difference, etc.), these may produce different results.
Example.
$\qquad(1 - 2) - 3 = - 4$,
whereas
$\qquad1 - (2 - 3) = 0$
Terminology. In functional programming, combining the values of a recursive data structure (like a list here) is often called a fold (or sometimes a reduce operation).
The left-to-right strategy is a left fold, and the right-to-left strategy a right fold.
Note. A right fold on a list mimics its recursive structure. For that reason, right folds are sometimes easier to understand than left folds.
Note. Folds are not limited to lists, but apply to other recursive structures, notably trees.
in Haskell #

title: Linear recursion
content:
Linear recursion # In this section, we will mostly use Haskell code as illustrations.
Execution # Example.
The following function sums the elements of a list of integers
sum :: [Int] -> Int sum [] = 0 sum (x : xs) = x + sum xs Let us clarify how an expression like sum [1,2,3] is evaluated.
Fist, recall that [1,2,3] is syntactic sugar for 1 : (2 : (3 : [])).
Therefore when the pattern (x : xs) (line XXX) is matched against [1,2,3]:
x matches 1, and xs matches 2 : (3 : []) (or equivalently, [2,3]) So the evaluation of sum [1,2,3] requires evaluating sum [2,3], recursively.
Besides, the + operation is performed after termination of the recursive call.
So the following sequence of events takes place:
the evaluation of sum [1,2,3] starts $\quad$ the evaluation of sum [2,3] starts $\quad\quad$ the evaluation of sum [3] starts $\quad\quad\quad$ the evaluation of sum [] starts $\quad\quad\quad$ the evaluation of sum [] ends and returns 0 $\quad\quad$ the evaluation of sum [3] ends and returns 3 + sum [] (i.e. 3) $\quad$ the evaluation of sum [2,3] ends and returns 2 + sum [3] (i.e. 5) the evaluation of sum [1,2,3] end ands returns 1 + sum [2,3] (i.e. 6) After Step 4, the call stack is:
sum[] sum[3] sum[2,3] sum[1,2,3] Writing a recursive function # Understanding the execution of a recursive program (as we did above) is mostly useful for debugging.
In order to write a recursive program/function, one can very often use a simpler, high-level methodology:
Methodology. To solve a problem recursively:
(Base case). If the input $I$ is minimal (e.g. of size 0 or size 1), then solve the problem directly. (Inductive case). Otherwise: Decompose $I$ into one or several smaller input(s) $I_1$, .., $I_k$. Solve the problem for $I_1$, .., $I_k$ by performing recursive calls. Use the results(s) of the recursive calls to solve the problem for $I$. In other words, in the inductive case, solve the problem for $I$ under the assumption that it can be solved for $I_1$, .., $I_k$.
Example. Let us reproduce the function above.
sum :: [Int] -> Int -- base case (the input list has size 0) sum [] = 0 -- inductive case (the input list has size n + 1) sum (x : xs) = x + sum xs In this example, the input $I$ is a list.
In the base case ($I$ is the empty list), we solve the problem directly by returning 0 In the inductive case, $I$ is of the form $a \colon I’$, where $a$ is an integer and $I’$ is a list. In this case, the sub-list $I’$ is our (unique) smaller input. We: solve the problem for $I’$ by calling the function sum recursively, and use the result to solve the problem for the full list $I$, assuming that the recursive call is correct. Hint. For pure functions, we can always assume that a recursive call is correct, as long as its input is smaller than the current input $I$.
Correctness of the whole algorithm holds by induction on the size of the input.
Example.
In the example above:
If the input list has size $0$ (i.e. if it is empty), then the function is trivially correct. If the input list has size $1$, then the recursive call (Line XXX) is correct (since it takes as input a list of size $0$), so the function is correct, If the input list has size $2$, then the recursive call (Line XXX) is correct (since it takes as input a list of size $1$), so the function is correct, … If the input list has size $n+1$, then the recursive call (Line XXX) is correct (since it takes as input a list of size $n$), so the function is correct. Hint. When the input $I$ is a list, the tail of $I$ is often a good candidate for a recursive call.
Exercise. implement in Haskell the function length :: [a] -> Int that maps a list to its length.
Exercise. Implement in Haskell the function reverse :: [a] -> [a] that maps a list to the reversed list. For instance, reverse [2,3,1,3] evaluates to [3,1,3,2].
Exercise.
Implement in Haskell the function zip :: [a] -> [b] -> [(a,b)]
Possible solution.
zip :: [a] -> [b] -> [(a,b)] zip _ [] = [] zip [] _ = [] zip (x : xs) (y : ys) = (x,y) : zip xs ys Exercise. Implement in Haskell the function endsWith :: a -> [a] -> Bool where endsWith x xs is True iff xs ends with x.
-- empty list endsWith _ [] = False -- list of size 1 endsWith x [y] = x == y -- list of size > 1 endsWith x (_ : xs) = endsWith x xs Exercise.
Implement in Haskell the function contains :: a -> [a] -> Bool where contains x ys is True iff x appears in xs
contains _ [] = False contains x (y : ys) = x == y || contains x ys ` Exercise. Implement in Haskell a _recursive version_ of the functions `map :: (a -> b) -> [a] -> [b]` and `filter :: (a -> Bool) -> [a] -> [a]` that we have [seen earlier](). `` Exercise. Implement in Haskell the functions `any :: (a -> Bool) -> [a] -> Bool` and `all :: (a -> Bool) -> [a] -> Bool` that we have [seen earlier](). Possible solutions. ```haskell any :: (a -> Bool) -> [a] -> Bool any _ [] = False any f (x : xs) = f x || any f xs all :: (a -> Bool) -> [a] -> Bool all _ [] = True all f (x : xs) = f x && all f xs Carrying intermediate values # In both cases, we need an auxiliary recursive method.
Accumulators # Example.
Implement the function firstPos :: a -> [a] -> Int where firstPos x xs is:
0 is x does not appear in xs, and the position of the first occurrence of x in xs otherwise (assuming that positions start at 1) multiple return values # Exercise.
[!TODO] Consider doing this exercise in Java instead
Implement the function posTailLength :: [Bool] -> Int that maps a list of Boolean values to the number of consecutive True values at the end of the list.
Solution
posTailLength :: [Bool] -> Int posTailLength xs = first (posTailLengthTrec xs) -- the return type carries an extra value posTailLengthRec :: [Bool] -> (Int, Bool) posTailLengthRec [] = 0 posTailLengthRec (False : xs) = (False, second (posTailLengthTRec xs)) posTailLengthRec (True : xs) | (True, v) <- posTailLengthTrec xs = (True, v + 1) | (False, v) <- posTailLengthTrec xs = (False, v) Tail recursion # An example in Java #

title: Set, list, tuple
content:
Set, list, tuple # Set # A set can be (informally) viewed as a collection of elements with no duplicate and in no specific order.
Examples.
$\{2,5\}$ and $\{5,2\}$ are the same set, $\{2,5,2\}$ is not a set. Power set # Definition. The power set $\mathcal{P}(X)$ of a set $X$ is the set of all subsets of $X$.
Example.
$\qquad\qquad \mathcal{P}(\{a,b,c\}) = \Big\{ \{\}, \{a\}, \{b\}, \{c\}, \{a,b\}, \{a,c\}, \{b,c\}, \{a,b,c\} \Big\} $
If $X$ is finite with size $n$, then $\mathcal{P}(X)$ has size $2^n$.
Notation. $2^X$ is an alternative notation for $\mathcal{P}(X)$.
Product of sets # Definition. The product $X_1 \times X_2$ of two sets $X_1$ and $X_2$ is the set of all pairs $(x_1, x_2)$ such that $x_1 \in X_1$ and $x_2 \in X_2$.
Example.
$\qquad\qquad \{a,b\} \times \{1,2,3\} = \{ \ (a,1), (a,2), (a,3), (b,1), (b,2), (b,3)\ \} $
Notation. Similarly to $X_1 \times X_2$:
$X_1 \times X_2 \times X_3$ denotes the set of all triples $(x_1, x_2, x_3)$ such that $x_1 \in X_1,\ x_2 \in X_2$ and $x_3 \in X_3$. $X_1 \times .. \times X_k$ denotes the set of all tuples $(x_1, .., x_k)$ such that $x_1 \in X_1,..,\ x_k \in X_k$. Notation.
$X^2$ is sometimes used for $X \times X$, $X^3$ is sometimes used for $X \times X \times X$, etc. Set comprehension # Example. If $X$ is a set of numbers, then the expression
$\qquad\{x^2 \mid x \in X,\ x \ge 5\}$
means
$\qquad$ all $x^2$ such that $x \in X$ and $x \ge 5$.
This is the set that consists of all numbers greater or equal to $5$ in $X$, each squared.
Syntax and meaning. A set comprehension is an expression of the form $\{\alpha \mid \varphi_1, \ldots, \varphi_n \}$, where:
$\alpha$ is a expression (like $x^2$), and each $\varphi_i$ is a Boolean condition (like $x \in X$). It describes the set of all $\alpha$’s such that the conditions $\varphi_1, \ldots, \varphi_n$ hold (altogether).
Terminology. In a set comprehension, the symbol $|$ means “such that”.
Exercise Exercise. Complete the table below (recall that $\mathbb{Z}$ is the set of integers, and $\mathbb{N}$ the set of integers $\ge 0$).
set comprehension corresponding set $\{x^2 \mid x \in \{2,3,5,7\},\ x \ge 5\}$ $\{25, 49\}$ $\{ - x \mid x \in \mathbb{Z},\ x \le 0\}$ $\{ x + y \mid x \in \{3,6\},\ y \in \mathbb{N},\ 4 \le y \le 6,\ y < x \}$ Solution Set comprehension corresponding set $\{x^2 \mid x \in \{2,3,5,7\},\ x \ge 5\}$ $\{25, 49\}$ $\{ - x \mid x \in \mathbb{Z},\ x \le 0\}$ $\mathbb{N}$ $\{ x + y \mid x \in \{3,6\},\ y \in \mathbb{N},\ 4 \le y \le 6,\ y < x \}$ $\{10,11\}$ List, Tuple # List # Definition. A list is a (possibly infinite) sequence.
As opposed to a set, a list may contain duplicates, and is ordered.
Example. The following three lists are different:
$1,1,4$ $1,4$ $1,4,1$ Terminology. In programming languages, the term “list” is often reserved to the ones whose elements have the same type. For instance, a list of integers or a list of strings.
Tuple # Definition. A tuple is a finite list.
A tuple is often written in parentheses.
Example.
$\ ()$ is the 0-tuple or empty tuple,
$\ (5,3)$ is a 2-tuple or pair,
$\ (\mathit{“banana”},5)$ is another pair,
$\ (\mathit{“tomato”},5,\mathit{“tomato”})$ is a 3-tuple or triple, etc.
An $n$-tuple can also be viewed as an element of a product of $n$ sets.
Examples.
$ (5,3) \in \mathbb{N}\times \mathbb{N}$ $ (5,3) \in \mathbb{Z}\times \mathbb{N}$ $ (5,\sqrt{3}) \in \mathbb{N}\times \mathbb{R}$ $ (5,\sqrt{3}) \not\in \mathbb{N}\times \mathbb{N}$ $ (\mathit{“tomato”},5,\mathit{“tomato”}) \in \mathsf{String} \times \mathbb{N} \times \mathsf{String} \\$ Exercise Complete the table below
expression is a set $\{a, b\}$ yes $\{a, b, a\}$ no $\{\}$ $\{\{\}\}$ $\{\{\},\{a\}\}$ $\{\{a\},\{a\}\}$ $\{\{a\},\{a,b\}\}$ $\{\{a,b\},\{b,a\}\}$ $(\{a,b\})$ $\{(a,b)\}$ $\{(a, b), (a, b)\}$ $\{(a, b), (b, a)\}$ Solution expression is a set $\{a, b\}$ yes $\{a, b, a\}$ no $\{\}$ yes $\{\{\}\}$ yes $\{\{\},\{a\}\}$ yes $\{\{a\},\{a\}\}$ no $\{\{a\},\{a,b\}\}$ yes $\{\{a,b\},\{b,a\}\}$ no $(\{a,b\})$ no $\{(a,b)\}$ yes $\{(a, b), (a, b)\}$ no $\{(a, b), (b, a)\}$ yes

title: Tokens
content:
Tokens # Syntax. Here are some basic elements of a Haskell program:
meaning syntax examples variable name starts with a lower case letter x, f, remainder, maxValue function name starts with a lower case letter f, square, applyBoolOp type starts with an upper case letter Integer, Char, Bool type variable starts with a lower case letter a, type, baseType constructor starts with a capital letter Cons, CityC, True

title: Type variable
content:
Type variable # A (regular) variable stands for an underspecified value. Similarly, a type variable stands for an underspecified type.
Benefit. Type variables let us write functions/methods that apply to different types, without compromising type safety.
Example. Consider a function half that maps a list to its first half (say rounded down if the list has odd length).
The implementation of this function is identical for:
a list of strings, a list of integers, a list of Boolean values, etc. A type variable lets us declare such a function
in Haskell: -- `a` is a type variable in this declaration half :: [a] -> [a] or in Java: /* will be revealed later */ In Haskell # Syntax. In Haskell, a type variable starts with a lowercase letter (like a regular variable). By convention, the first letters of the alphabet (a, b, c, etc.) are often used as type variables.
Examples.
The type variable a stands for any type, [a] is the type of lists, a function with type [a] -> [a] maps a list to a list of the same type, a function with type (a, a) -> Bool maps a pair of elements of the same type to a Boolean value. Example. The Haskell Prelude provides two (partial) functions head and tail, implemented as follows. Observe the type declarations in these definitions.
-- Maps an nonempty list to its head. Throws an error if the list is empty. head :: [a] -> a head (x:_) = x head [] = error "Prelude.head: empty list" -- Maps an nonempty list to its tail. Throws an error if the list is empty. tail :: [a] -> [a] tail (_:xs) = xs tail [] = error "Prelude.tail: empty list" Benefits # Example (continued).
Generality # The function head above can be applied to any nonempty list, regardless of whether it consists of integers, strings, etc.
For instance:
the expression head [3,1,2] is valid (and evaluates to 3), and the expression head ["because","we","can"] is also a valid (and evaluates to "because"). Type safety # The type declaration head :: [a] -> a ensures that the following program does not compile:
even :: Int -> Bool even :: x = x % 2 == 0 main :: IO () {- Invalid expression: `head ["because","we","can"]` evaluates to a `String`, whereas `even` expects an `Int`. -} main = print $ even (head ["because","we","can"]) Warning. Recall that the empty list has type [a] for any type a. So the following program (unfortunately) compiles:
main :: IO () main = print $ head [] The program will only throw an error at run time (like any other attempt to apply the function head to the empty list).
This is a limitation of Haskell’s type system (this error cannot be captured at compile time).

title: Base types
content:
Base types # A base type in Haskell intuitively represents a set of values (like in Java, and many other languages).
Here are a few native base types:
type meaning syntax of constants in Haskell Char a Unicode character, like b, B, 5, _ or \t. 'b', 'B', '5', '_' or '\t' (single quotes) String a sequence of Unicode characters, like banana or f?#\t5 "banana" or "f?#\t5" (double quotes) Bool a Boolean value True or False Integer an arbitrary integer (i.e. an element of $\mathbb{Z}$) -42, 0 or 3 Int an integer within a certain range, from $- 2^{63}$ to $2^{63} -1$ (i.e. 64 bits) by default. -42, 0 or 3 Float a 32 bits floating-point number -42, 4472, 4472.0 or 4472.1357 Double a 64 bits floating-point number -42, 4472, 4472.0, 4472.1357 or 4472.13595499958

title: List comprehension
content:
List comprehension # We have already seen the syntax for set comprehension.
in Haskell # Haskell (but also Python for instance) provides a similar syntax for lists.
Example. In Haskell, if xs is a list of numbers, then we can write
$\qquad$ [x^2 | x <- xs, x >= 5]
This expression evaluates to the list of all x^2 such that x is in xs and x >= 5.
Terminology. In the example above, the expression x <- xs is called a generator.
A list comprehension may have several generators.
Example. Here is a list comprehension with two generators:
$\qquad$ [x + y | x <- xs, y <- ys]
It evaluates to the list of all x + y such that x is in xs and y is in ys.
Warning. For a list (as opposed to set) comprehension:
the order of the output list reflects the order of the initial list(s), the output list may contain duplicates. Examples.
[x^2 | x <- [3,5,2]] evaluates to [9,25,4] [x^2 | x <- [-3,3]] evaluates to [9,9] [x^2 | x <- [3,5,2], x < 5] evaluates to [9,4] [x + y | x <- [3,5,2], y <- [2,3]] evaluates to [5,6,7,8,4,5] Exercise
The function sum maps a list of numbers to their sum. For instance, sum [2,3,6] evaluates to 11.
What does the following function do?
myFunction:: [Integer] -> Integer myFunction xs = sum [1 | x <- xs] Solution
It returns the length of the input list.
For instance
Exercise
What does the following function do?
myFunction:: [[Integer]] -> [Integer] myFunction xss = [x | xs <- xss, x <- xs ] Solution
It concatenates all lists in an input list of lists.
For instance
myFunction [[1,3], [2,5], [1,3,4]] evaluates to [1,3,2,5,1,3,4]
Exercise
Write a function firstsIfSecPos that maps a list of pairs of integers to a list that consists of the first element of each pair, if the second element is $\ge 0$.
For instance firstsIfSecPos [(3,4), (5,-2), (-3,6), (1,3)] evaluates to [3, -3, 1]
Solution
firstsIfSecPos:: [(Integer, Integer)] -> [Integer] firstsIfSecPos ps = [x | (x,y) <- ps, y >= 0 ]

title: Product type
content:
Product type # A product type can be viewed as a simplistic class, and its instances as simplistic objects.
in Haskell # Syntax. Like an enumerated type, a product type in Haskell can be declared with the keyword data, as follows
data <product type name> = <definition> There are two alternative syntaxes for this <definition>: either a tuple type, or a definition with a constructor.
Definition as a tuple type # Example. The declaration below defines a product type Interval.
An Interval consists of two integers.
-- a temporal interval, represented as two timestamps (start and end) data Interval = (Int, Int) In this definition, (Int, Int) is the type of pairs of integers.
Example. The expression below define the product type Signal.
A Signal consists of:
an Interval, and a value for the enumerated type Light seen earlier. -- a traffic signalling event, represented as a temporal interval and the light (Green, Yellow or Red) during that interval data Signal = (Interval, Light) In this definition, (Interval, Light) is the type of all pairs with:
a first element of type Interval, and a second element of type Light. Instances # Syntax (reminder). A tuple in Haskell is written in parentheses.
Examples. Consider the two product types that we just defined:
data Interval = (Int, Int) data Signal = (Interval, Light) Then:
the pair (5,8) has type Interval, the pair ((5,8), Red) has type Signal. Definition with a constructor # Haskell provides another syntax to define a product type, by means of a constructor. As we will see later, when combined with sum types, this syntax allows:
concise pattern matching, and recursive types. So this is often the preferred way to define a product type.
Syntax. A product type with constructor can be declared as
data <product type name> = <constructor> <first type name> ... <last type name> where the constructor and each type name starts with a capital letter.
Syntax. The constructor can be identical to the product type name. This is a widely adopted convention.
Example (continued). The type Interval seen above can alternatively be declared:
data Interval = Interval Int Int In this declaration, the product type and its constructor have the same name (Interval). This is not mandatory (only a convention).
Example (continued). The type Signal seen above can alternatively be declared:
data Signal = Signal Interval Light Instance # An instance of such a type is created with its constructor (as in Java).
Examples.
Consider the two product types that we have just defined:
data Interval = Interval Int Int data Signal = Signal Interval Light Then:
the expression Interval 5 10 has type Interval,
the expression Signal (Interval 5 10) Red has type Signal.
Pattern matching # A constructor can be used to create complex patterns.
Example. In the program below:
the function getLight maps a signal to its light (i.e. Green, Yellow or Red), the function isLenghty maps a signal to True if its interval is longer than 3. data Interval = Interval Int Int data Signal = Signal Interval Light getLight :: Signal -> Light getLight Signal _ l = l isLenghty :: Signal -> Bool isLenghty Signal (Interval b e) _ = e - b > 3 Warning. These patterns do not use the name of product type, but the constructor. So when the two differ, make sure to use the latter.
Example. In the following example, the product type Interval is defined with a constructor called MyConstructor. The function isValid reflects this distinction:
its type (Interval -> Bool) uses the product type, whereas the pattern MyConstructor b e uses the constructor. data Interval = MyConstructor Int Int -- checks whether a temporal interval is valid isValid :: Interval -> Bool isValid MyConstructor b e = b < e Exercise Consider the following program:
data Interval = Interval Int Int data Signal = Signal Interval Light aFunction :: [Signal] -> [Interval] aFunction [] = [] aFunction (Signal i Red : ss) = i : aFunction ss aFunction (_ : ss) = aFunction ss What does the following expression evaluate to? aFunction [ Signal (Interval 0 2) Red, Signal (Interval 2 3) Green, Signal (Interval 3 4) Orange, Signal (Interval 4 6) Red, ] More generally, what does the function aFunction do? Solution [(Interval 0,2), (Interval 4,6)] It maps a list of signals to the intervals for which the light was red. Exercise Same two questions as above, for the function anotherFunction below (recall the higher-order functions filter and map seen earlier).
data Interval = Interval Int Int data Signal = Signal Interval Light anotherFunction :: [Signal] -> [Interval] anotherFunction = (map \(Signal i _) -> i) . (filter \(Signal _ l) -> l == Red) Solution It is equivalent to the function of the previous exercise.
Unary constructor # Syntax. A constructor can also create “tuples” that consist of a single value. This will be useful to define recursive types.
Example. The following type is valid in Haskell.
data DummyType = DummyType Int Record-like notation # Syntax. Haskell provides an alternative notation for a product type with constructor, using attribute names. This is very similar to structures in C or records in Java. For instance:
data Interval = Interval { start :: Int, end :: Int } data Signal = Signal { interval :: Interval, light :: Light } For details, we refer to XXX.
We will not use this syntax in this course (but feel free to use it during the exam).

title: Anonymous function
content:
Anonymous function # We have seen in the dedicated section that a function can be written anonymously, as a lambda expression.
In Haskell # Syntax. “$\lambda x.$” in Haskell is written \x ->
Example. The two following functions are equivalent
aFunction :: Integer -> Integer aFunction x = x + 2 theSameFunction :: Integer -> Integer theSameFunction = \x -> x + 2

title: Basic syntax of Haskell
content:
Basic syntax of Haskell # This chapter provides a (very incomplete) introduction to the elementary syntax of Haskell:
tokens base types native functions and operators tuple basic function definition basic program list Some core features of the language are introduced in other chapters (together with their counterparts in Java):
anonymous function currying pattern matching guards conditional statements list comprehension recursion composite type parameterized type functor and monad

title: Currying
content:
Currying # Functional program traditionally define functions in curried form (for a definition, we refer to the dedicated section).
In Haskell # Syntax # A Haskell function can (in theory) take a tuple as argument.
Example.
exp :: (Integer, Integer) -> Integer exp (x,y) = x^y However, functions in curried form are usually preferred, because they are more versatile.
Example. The function above in curried form can be written:
exp :: Integer -> (Integer -> Integer) exp = \x -> (\y -> x^y) or equivalently:
exp :: Integer -> (Integer -> Integer) exp x = \y -> x^y or equivalently (and more commonly):
exp :: Integer -> (Integer -> Integer) (exp x) y = x^y Syntax. Parentheses in the above example are redundant, because:
the -> operator associates to the right, and function application associates to the left. So this function is normally written:
exp :: Integer -> Integer -> Integer exp x y = x^y Exercise Write in Haskell the function $apply(f,x)$ in curried form, where:
$f$ is a function that maps strings to Boolean values, $x$ is a string, $apply(f,x) = f(x)$. Possible solution apply :: (String -> Bool) -> String -> Bool apply f x = f x Alternative solution apply :: (String -> Bool) -> String -> Bool apply f = \x -> f x Alternative solution apply :: (String -> Bool) -> String -> Bool apply = \f -> \x -> f x Exercise Write in Haskell the curried form of the function $applySwapped(x,f)$, which is identical to the function above, but where $f$ and $x$ are swapped.
Possible solution applySwapped :: String -> (String -> Bool) -> Bool applySwapped x f = f x Exercise Consider the following Haskell program.
aFunction :: Integer -> Integer -> Integer aFunction x y = y - x anotherFunction = aFunction 1 Which of the following types is correct for the function anotherFunction? Integer -> Integer -> Integer (Integer -> Integer) -> Integer Integer -> Integer What does this function do? Solution Integer -> Integer
It maps an integer y to y - 1.
Exercise Consider the following Haskell program, where the function unicodeCodePoint:: Char -> Int maps a character to its Unicode number.
aFunction i j f c = anotherFunction i j (f c) anotherFunction :: Int -> Int -> Int -> Int anotherFunction i j k = (i * j) + k main = print $ aFunction 10 3 unicodeCodePoint 'V' The Unicode number of V is 118. So what does the method main print?
Which of the following types is correct for the method aFunction?
Int -> Int -> Char -> Int -> Char -> Int (Int -> Int) -> Char -> Int -> Char -> Int Int -> (Int -> Char) -> Int -> Char -> Int Int -> Int -> (Char -> Int) -> Char -> Int Int -> Int -> Char -> (Int -> Char) -> Int (Int -> Int) -> (Char -> Int) -> Char -> Int (Int -> Int) -> (Char -> Int) -> Int -> Char -> Int (Int -> Int) -> (Char -> Int -> Char) -> Int (Int -> Int) -> (Char -> Int -> Char) -> Char -> Int (Int -> Int) -> (Char -> Int -> Char) -> Int -> Char -> Int Solution 148 Int -> Int -> (Char -> Int) -> Char -> Int Exercise Write in curried form the function $\textit{applyAndCompose}(x,f,g)$, where:
$x$ is an integer (i.e. $x \in \mathbb{Z}$), $f$ is a function $f’\colon \mathbb{Z} \times \text{String} \to \mathbb{Z}$ in curried form, $g$ is a function that maps integers to Booleans, and $\textit{applyAndCompose}(x,f,g)$ is the composition of $f(x)$ and $g$. Possible solution applyAndCompose :: Integer -> (Integer -> String -> Integer) -> (Integer -> Bool) -> String -> Bool applyAndCompose x f g = g . (f x) Infix notation # Some native Haskell functions are written in infix notation. These are often binary operators, like + (sum of two numbers) or && (Boolean conjunction). These function are implicitly in curried form.
Example. The function && has type Bool -> Bool -> Bool.
So we can call an infix function with its “first argument” only (like any function in curried form).
Example. 2 - is the function that subtracts a number from two.
In other words, the two functions below are equivalent:
myFunction :: Integer -> Integer myFunction x = 2 - x theSameFunction :: Integer -> Integer theSameFunction = 2 - Surprisingly, an infix function in Haskell can also be called with its “second argument” only (whereas a prefix function cannot).
Example. - 2 is the function that subtracts two from a number.
In other words, the two functions below are equivalent:
myFunction :: Integer -> Integer myFunction x = x - 2 theSameFunction :: Integer -> Integer theSameFunction = - 2 Infix to prefix # An infix function can be used as a regular (i.e. prefix) one, by surrounding it with parentheses.
Examples.
infix form equivalent prefix form 2 + 3 (+) 2 3 True && True (&&) True True Example. The two functions below are equivalent:
myFunction :: Integer -> Integer myFunction = 2 - theSameFunction :: Integer -> Integer theSameFunction = (-) 2 This is helpful in contexts where the infix form cannot be used.
Example. We can pass the function && to another function, by surrounding it with parentheses.
applyBoolOp :: (Bool -> Bool -> Bool) -> Bool -> Bool -> Bool applyBoolOp f x y = f x y main = print $ applyBoolOp (&&) True False We can also define an infix function. In this case, the type declaration must use parentheses.
Example. The program below define a function myOp in infix form.
(myOp) :: Integer -> Integer -> Bool x myOp y = x > 0 && x < y main = print $ 2 myOp 4 Prefix to infix # Conversely, a curried function in prefix form can be used as an infix one, by surrounding it with `.
Examples.
prefix form equivalent infix form exp 2 3 2 `exp` 3 isPrefixOf "foo" "foobar" "foo" `isPrefixOf` "foobar" This allows us to call a function in curried form with its “second argument” only.
Example. The two functions below are equivalent.
myFunction :: String -> Bool myFunction x = isPrefixOf x "fooBar" theSameFunction :: String -> Bool theSameFunction = `isPrefixOf` "fooBar"

title: Evaluation
content:
Evaluation # Evaluation for this course is based on:
a final exam assignments Your final mark is the best of the two following:
exam mark, or $0.7\ \cdot\ $ exam mark $ +\ 0.3 \ \cdot\ $ assignments mark. Exam # The exam consists of:
multiple choice questions, and programming exercises. These are direct applications of the content of the course.
No internet access is allowed.
Assignments # There will be 7 or 8 assignments for this course. These are standard programming exercises, related to notions seen during the lectures.
We highly encourage you do the assignments, on your own. This will prepare you to the final exam.
Release and submission # The assignments are individual (do not submit the code of another student). The release of each assignment is announced via Teams.
Assignments are submitted via Codeboard. You can submit an assignment multiple times (but only before its deadline).
Note. In order to submit your assignments (and your project), you will need a Codeboard account. If you do not have one already, please create one and communicate it to the lecturer, preferably via Teams. Make sure that you use a single account for all your assignments.
Marking and lab attendance # The labs are (mostly) dedicated to the assignments. In particular, some labs are (partly) dedicated to correcting an assignment. These will be announced via Teams.
Each assignment is automatically graded. However, by default, your mark will not be registered. In addition to submitting an assignment, you have to to attend the lab dedicated to its correction. During this lab, you may be (randomly) chosen to explain your solution to an exercise. If you do not understand the code that you submitted, then you will not be granted points fore this assignment.

title: Function
content:
Function # A function $f\colon X \to Y$ maps each element $x$ of its domain $X$ to an element $f(x)$ of its codomain $Y$.
Terminology. A function is also called a map.
Examples.
the function $f\colon \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{N}$ defined by $\qquad f(x) = x^2$
maps each integer to its square.
the function $g\colon \mathbb{N} \to \mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})$ defined by $\qquad g(x) = \{y \mid y \in \mathbb{N},\ y < x\}$
maps each natural number to the set of natural numbers that precede it.
Injection, bijection, surjection # Definition. A function $f\colon X \to Y$ is:
injective (or an injection) is no two elements in its domain have the same image, i.e. if for all $ x_1, x_2 \in X$, $\qquad \qquad x_1 \neq x_2$ implies $f(x_1) \neq f(x_2)$
surjective (or a surjection) if every element in its codomain has a preimage, i.e. if $\qquad \qquad$ for each $y \in Y$, there is a $x \in X$ such that $y = f(x)$.
bijective (or a bijection) if it is injective and surjective. Anonymous function # Example. Consider the function
$$f\colon \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{N} \text{ defined by } f(x) = x^2$$
This is (arguably) the same function as
$$g\colon \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{N} \text{ defined by } g(x) = x^2$$
So the name of these function ($f$ or $g$) is in a certain sense irrelevant. They can be both described as “the function that maps an integer $x$ to $x^2$”.
Notation (borrowed from lambda calculus).
Let $f(x) = D$ be a function definition (for instance, $D$ could be $x^2$).
Then $\lambda x.D$ is an alternative notation for $f$.
In other words, $f$ can be viewed as an alias for $\lambda x.D$.
Example (continued).
$\lambda x.x^2 \colon \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{N}$ is the function that maps an integer $x$ to $x^2$.
It is equivalent to the functions $f$ and $g$ above.
For instance,
$$(\lambda x.x^2)(3) = f(3) = g(3) = 9$$
Exercise Evaluate the following expressions:
$(\lambda x.2x)(5)$ $(\lambda x.3x)\big((\lambda y.y+2)(1)\big)$ Solution expression value $(\lambda x.2x)(5)$ 10 $(\lambda x.3x)((\lambda y.y+2)(1))$ 9 Terminology. An anonymous function is also called a lambda abstraction.
Higher-order function # The domain of a function may consist of functions.
Example. We can define a function $\textit{isInjective}$ that maps a function $\mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{N}$ to $1$ if it is injective, and to $0$ otherwise.
For instance:
$\textit{isInjective}(\lambda x.x+1) = 1$ $\textit{isInjective}(\lambda x.x^2) = 0$ The co-domain of a function may also consist of functions.
Examples. Consider the function $f\colon \mathbb{N} \to (\mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N})$ defined by
$\qquad f(x) = \lambda y. x^y$
Then:
$f(2)$ is the function $\lambda y.2^y$, or equivalently $g(y) = 2^y$, $f(3)$ is the function $\lambda y.3^y$, or equivalently $h(y) = 3^y$, etc. Exercise Which of the following definitions are equivalent?
$f = \lambda x.(\lambda y.x - y)$ $f(x) = \lambda y.x - y$ $f(z) = \lambda x.z - x$ $f(x)(y) = x - y$ $f(y)(x) = x - y$ Solution $1,2,3$ and $4$.
Currying # Intuition. Consider a function $f\colon X \times Y \to Z$.
This function takes as argument a pair of elements.
For instance, this function could be $f\colon \mathbb{N} \times \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$, defined by
$\qquad f(x,y) = x + y$
If we know the values of $x$ and $y$, then we can evaluate $f(x,y)$. For instance, if $x$ is $2$ and $y$ is $3$, then
$\qquad f(x,y) = 2 + 3 = 5$
Now what if we only know the value of $x$?
In this case, we cannot evaluate $f(x,y)$.
Instead, the best approximation of $f(x,y)$ that we can produce is a function that already knows the value of $x$ and is only “waiting” for the value of $y$.
In our example, this function is:
$\qquad f_2(y) = 2 + y$
More generally, if we only know the value $a$ of $x$, then the best approximation of $f(x,y)$ that we can produce is the function
$\qquad f_a(y) = a + y$
Now the function $\bar{f} \colon X \to (Y \to Z)$ defined by
$\qquad \bar{f}(x) = f_x$
is called $f$ in curried form.
Intuitively, $\bar{f}$:
takes a value for the first argument of $f$, and returns a function that expects a value for the second argument of $f$. So in our example, $f$ in curried form is
$\qquad \bar{f} = \lambda x.(\lambda y.x + y)$
or equivalently
$\qquad \bar{f}(x) = \lambda y.x + y$
or equivalently
$\qquad \bar{f}(x)(y) = x + y$
Definition. If $f\colon X \times Y \to Z$ is a function, then $f$ in curried form is the function
$\qquad \bar{f}\colon X \to (Y \to Z)$
defined by
$\qquad \bar{f} = \lambda x.(\lambda y. f(x,y))$
or equivalently
$\qquad \bar{f}(x) = \lambda y. f(x,y)$
or equivalently
$\qquad \bar{f}(x)(y) = f(x,y)$
Exercise Write the following functions in curried form
$f(x,y) = y^x$ $f(h,x) = h(x)$ $f(x,y) = \lambda z.x^y + z$ Solution $f(x,y) = y^x$ in curried form is the function $\bar{f}$ defined by
$\qquad \bar{f}(x)(y) = y^x$
$f(h,x) = h(x)$ in curried form is the function $\bar{f}$ defined by:
$\qquad \bar{f}(h)(x) = h(x)$
$f(x,y) = \lambda z.x^y + z$ in curried form is the function $\bar{f}$ defined by:
$\qquad \bar{f}(x)(y) = \lambda z.x^y + z$
Generalization. Currying is not limited to functions over pairs: this extends to functions over arbitrary tuples. Let us define it for triples (the case of quadruples, quintuples, etc. is analogous).
If $f\colon W \times X \times Y \to Z$ is a function, then $f$ in curried form is the function $\bar{f}$ defined by
$\qquad \bar{f}(w)(x)(y) = f(w,x,y)$
where function application associates to the left, i.e. $\bar{f}(w)(x)(y)$ means $(\bar{f}(w)(x))(y)$.
Composition # Definition. The composition of two functions
$\qquad f\colon X \to Y\qquad$ and $\qquad g\colon Y \to Z$
is the function
$\qquad g \circ f\colon X \to Z$
defined as
$\qquad(g \circ f)(x) = g(f(x))$
For instance, if $$f(x) = x - 2$$ and $$g(y) = 3y,$$ then $$(g \circ f)(x) = 3(x - 2)$$
Exercise Evaluate the following expressions
$\Big((\lambda x.3x) \circ(\lambda y.y+2)\Big)(1)$
$\Big(\big((\lambda x.(\lambda y.y-x))(1)\big) \circ (\lambda z.2z) \Big) (3)$
Solution expression value $\Big((\lambda x.3x) \circ(\lambda y.y+2)\Big)(1)$ 9 $\Big(\big((\lambda x.(\lambda y.y-x))(1)\big) \circ (\lambda z.2z) \Big) (3)$ 5

title: Higher order function
content:
Higher order function # This chapter covers an essential feature of functional languages: a function can take a function as argument and/or return a function. This enables currying, and is often paired with the usage of anonymous functions (both borrowed from lambda calculus).
We will illustrate these notions in Haskell, and how they have made their way to Java.

title: Higher order functions and lists
content:
Higher-order functions and lists # We introduce a few useful higher-order functions frequently used with lists.
In Haskell # filter # The function filter retains elements of a list that satisfy a certain condition.
It is defined as follows:
filter:: (a -> Bool) -> [a] -> [a] filter f xs = [ x | x <- xs, f x ] Example.
Recall the function even that we defined earlier, as follows:
even :: Integer -> Boolean even x = x % 2 == 0 Then filter even [1,4,6,4] evaluates to [4,6,4]
Equivalently, instead of defining the function even, we could inline it (as an anonymous function) in the expression above, i.e. we could write filter (\x -> x % 2 == 0) [1,4,6,4]
map # The function map applies a function to every element of a list.
It is defined as follows:
map :: (a -> b) -> [a] -> [b] map f xs = [ f x | x <- xs ] Examples.
map even [1,4,5,4] evaluates to [False, True, False, True] map (\x -> x % 2 == 0) [1,4,5,4] is equivalent map (+1) [1,4,5,4] evaluates to [2,5,6,5] flatmap # The function flatmap applies to every element of a list a function that produces a list, and concatenates the resulting list of lists.
It is defined as follows:
flatmap :: (a -> [b]) -> [a] -> [b] flatmap f xs = [ y | x <- xs, y <- f x ] all and any # The functions all and any have type (a -> Bool) -> [a] -> Bool.
The expression all f xs evaluates to true iff f x evaluates to true for every element x of the list xs. The expression any f xs evaluates to true iff f x evaluates to true for at least one element x of the list xs. Note. We will see how these two functions can be implemented in the section on recursion.
Examples.
all even [1,4,5,4] evaluates to False
all even [4,4,2] evaluates to True
all even [] evaluates to True
any even [1,4,5,2] evaluates to True
any even [1,3] evaluates to False
any even [] evaluates to False

title: Native functions
content:
Native functions # Syntax. Many native functions/operators (e.g. over numbers or Boolean values) have the same syntax in Haskell and Java. This includes equality (==), sum (+), product (*), modulo (%), etc.
We highlight below a few cases where the two syntaxes differ:
meaning syntax in Haskell example syntax in Java example inequality /= x /= 2 != x != 2 negation (NOT) not not x ! !x disjunction (OR) || x || y || (lazy evaluation) or | (full evaluation) x || y or x | y conjunction (AND) && x && y && (lazy evaluation) or & (full evaluation) x && y or x & y

title: Option type
content:
Option type # We introduce an important parameterized and composite type, available in many languages, under a variety of names (Maybe, Option, optional, Optional, Nullable, etc.)
We will see in the dedicated sections that such a type can also be made a functor, and (perhaps most importantly) a monad. For now, we will use it without these extra features.
Motivation # Problem. A programming language may not have a type that exactly fits the domain of a function that we want to implement.
Examples. A programming language may not have a type that exactly fits:
positive integers, integers $\neq 0$, ASCII characters, nonempty lists (or nonempty arrays, nonempty sets, etc.), pairs of distinct elements, phone numbers, invertible matrices, etc. Consequence. When we implement a function, we may need to handle inputs that should not be in its domain.
Examples.
A function that returns the head of a list may receive the empty list as input, a function that divides two numbers may receive 0 as input divisor, a function that maps a date to the average temperature in Bolzano may receive a future date as input, etc. null value and propagation # A common way for a function/method $f$ to handle non-supported inputs (without throwing an exception) consists in returning a special value. Depending on the language, this value may be null, nil, undefined, etc.
Consequence. A program that calls $f$ must take this scenario into consideration:
if $f$ returns null, take a special action (e.g. return null as well), otherwise proceed normally. Drawback. Programmers often forget to check whether a value is null.
Indeed, in most programming languages, the return type of $f$ does not indicate that the value may be null (for instance, in Java, a method with return type Integer may return null).
Non-handled null values are a notorious source of bugs (“null pointer exception”, “segmentation fault”, etc.).
Second drawback. Handling null values explicitly creates boilerplate code (made worse by the fact that handling a null value often consists in returning one).
Option type # Functional languages typically do not have null values. Instead, they rely on a special type (or sometimes several) for values that may be null.
These are often called option type or maybe type.
Benefits.
An option return type for a function $f$ signals that it may not return a value. So this case must be handled by the code that is calling $f$.
Option types can be paired with higher-order functions that propagate “missing values” automatically, thus reducing boilerplate code. We will introduce such functions in the sections on functors and monads
in Haskell # Haskell uses a parameterized option type called Maybe.
Declaration. An expression with type Maybe a may evaluate to either nothing, or a value of type a (where a is a type variable).
Accordingly, the type Maybe is declared in the Haskell Prelude as
data Maybe a = Nothing | Just a Example. The following function maps a nonempty list to its head, and the empty list to nothing.
maybeHead :: [a] -> Maybe a maybeHead [] = Nothing maybeHead (x : _) = Just x Exercise Implement the function maybeLast :: [a] -> Maybe a that maps a nonempty list to its last element, and the empty list to Nothing.
Possible solution maybeLast :: [a] -> Maybe a maybeLast [] = Nothing maybeLast [x] = Just x maybeLast (_ : xs) = maybeLast xs Exercise Implement the native function find :: (a -> Bool) -> [a] -> Maybe a so that
find f xs evaluates to:
the first element of xs for which f evaluates to True, and Nothing if there is no such element. Possible solution find :: (a -> Bool) -> [a] -> Maybe a find _ [] = Nothing find f (x : xs) | f x = Just x | otherwise = find f xs

title: Recursive type
content: Recursive types #

title: Sum type
content:
Sum type # A sum type represents values that can belong to a finite set of alternative (possibly complex) types.
in Haskell # Syntax. A sum type in Haskell is declared as
data <sum type name> = <first type definition> | ... | <last type definition> Example. In the following declarations:
Point is a product type, and Shape is a sum type. -- a point in the Euclidean plane, represented by its coordinates data Point = Point Double Double -- a shape, defined as either a triangle or a rectangle data Shape = Triangle Point Point Point | Rectangle Point Point Point Point Note. in Haskell, an enumerated type is a sum type.
More precisely, the alternative “values” of an enumerated type are nullary (a.k.a. 0-ary) constructors, i.e. constructors with no argument.
Pattern matching # Just like an enumerated type, a sum type provides alternative patterns.
Example (continued). Let us define a function perimeter that maps a Shape to its perimeter.
First, we define an auxiliary function distance (in curried form) that maps two points to their distance:
distance :: Point -> Point -> Double distance (Point x1 y1) (Point x2 y2) = sqrt ( (x2 - x1) ^ 2 + (y2 - y1) ^ 2 ) In this definition, observe the usage of the constructor Point to create the two patterns Point x1 y1 and Point x2 y2.
We can now define our main function:
perimeter :: Shape -> Double perimeter Triangle p1 p2 p3 = distance p1 p2 + distance p2 p3 + distance p1 p3 perimeter Rectangle p1 p2 p3 p4 = 2 * (distance p1 p2 + distance p2 p3) Here we created one pattern out of each alternative definition of the sum type Shape:
the pattern Triangle p1 p2 p3, and the pattern Rectangle p1 p2 p3 p4.

title: Termination
content:
Recursion and termination # Tail recursive function # In Haskell # Exercise.
The function failedReverse below seems similar to the function reverse seen in the previous section.
However, there is something wrong with it. Can you identify what it is?
failedReverse :: [Int] -> [Int] failedReverse [] = [] failedReverse (x : xs) = failedReverse (xs ++ [x]) main :: IO () main = print $ failedReverse [1,2,3] Solution.
The recursive call (Line XXX) is performed on a list that is not smaller than the input list.
As a consequence, if ys is a nonempty list, then the evaluation of failedReverse ys does not terminate.
Execution. The function failedReverse is tail-recursive. The compiler is able to transform it into a loop, which in this case does not terminate. So if we execute the program, it simply loops, indefinitely.
Stack overflow # In Haskell # Example. Here is another non-terminating program
failedId :: Int -> Int failedId 0 = 0 failedId x = failedId (x + 1) - 1 main :: IO () main = print $ failedId 3 Note. As opposed to the previous example, the function failedId here is not tail-recursive. The compiler is unable to transform it into a loop.
As a consequence, the call stack grows as follows
... etc failedId 8 failedId 7 failedId 6 failedId 5 failedId 4 failedId 3 main until its capacity is exhausted. When this happens, the program is interrupted with a stack overflow exception.
Definition. A stack overflow exception is thrown during the execution of a program if the capacity of the call stack is exhausted.
Warning. A stack overflow is usually due to a non-terminating recursive call. However, a non-terminating recursive call may not result in a stack overflow, as illustrated with our first example.
Lazy evaluation # In Haskell # Example. Recall the function take :: Int -> [a] -> [a] seen the previous section, where take k xs consists of the first k elements of the list xs (or the whole xs if it has less than k elements).
Now consider the following program
succs :: Integer -> [Integer] succs x = x : succs (x + 1) main :: IO () main = print $ take 5 (succs 3) Execution.
Intuitively, succs 3 is the (infinite) list of integers $\ge 3$, i.e. the list $3, 4, 5, 6, \ldots $
A naive evaluation strategy for the expression take 5 (lazy 3) would proceed in two steps:
first evaluate succs 3, and then retain the first 5 elements of the resulting list. Since the evaluation of succs 3 (a.k.a. Step 1) does not terminate, the whole program would not terminate either.
Instead, Haskell’s lazy evaluation allows a different strategy, where only the first 5 elements of lazy 3 are computed.
As a consequence, the program terminates and prints [3,4,5,6,7].
Example (continued).
Let us simplify the function main as follows:
main :: IO () main = print $ succs 3 Execution.
As expected, this new program does not terminate. However, thanks to lazy evaluation, the program can still print the elements succs 3 on the fly, as soon as thy are computed. This is analogous to processing a continuous stream of incoming data.
Infinite lists # Several built-in Haskell functions produce infinite lists (like the function succs above). They can be convenient to define functions that act on the $n$ first elements of a list, when $n$ is not known in advance.
Example. The Haslell Prelude provide the function iterate :: (a -> a) -> a -> [a], where iterate f x is the infintie list of iterative applications of f to x
For instance iterate (+1) x is equivalent to the function succs above.
Exercise.
Define the function repeat :: a -> [a] that repeats an elemtn infinitely many times.
Possible solution.
repeat :: a -> [a] repeat = iterate id Alternative solution.
repeat :: a -> [a] repeat x = x : repeat x

title: Content
content:
Content of the course # Prerequisites # Students are expected to have completed the Computer Programming course (INF/01 76203).
Therefore the following topics are not covered in this course:
variables, assignments, variable scope, basic data types (int, char, boolean, etc.). conditional statements (“if/then/else”) and loops, expression evaluation, methods/functions, arrays, basic syntax of Java, etc. Content # This is a beginner’s programming course, with a focus on:
(class-based) object oriented programming and functional programming. The main topics that we will cover are:
objects, classes and interfaces (inheritance, encapsulation, value vs reference, comparing objects, cloning objects, etc.), abstract data types (set, list, associative array, queue, etc.) and data structures (array, linked list, hash table, etc.), recursion, higher-order and anonymous functions (lambda calculus, currying, closures, etc.) and their applications (callback methods, Java streams, etc.), (higher-order) function types and generics, mutability, pure functions, lambda expressions and streams, multithreading. Due to limited time, many notions (such as asymptotic cost, hash tables or multi-threading) are only briefly introduced in this course. However, some of them will be further discussed in other courses of the bachelor.
Programming languages # We will use Java and Haskell as programming languages.
However, for a large part, the content of the course is not specific to these languages (although the syntax may be), but can be easily ported to other programming languages. Accordingly, for conciseness, some sections will use pseudocode.
Focus # Foundations # Most lectures put an emphasis on problem solving, rather than providing (mechanical) recipes.
Besides, the amount of (Java and Haskell) syntax provided in this course is on purpose limited (but sufficient for the assignments and exam).
Some lectures also focus on more abstract (albeit simple) notions. These includes elementary mathematical objects or structures: set, tuple, map, relation, preorder, etc.
The purpose is twofold:
generalize techniques seen in this course to programming languages other than Java and Haskell, provide a widely accepted vocabulary to document/explain your code. Note. What is considered good communication in computer science may differ from other disciplines. Emphasis is put on clarity, precision and conciseness.
Exercise Here is a (caricature of) a poorly documented method. Can you improve the method’s description and/or signature?
/** The algorithm looks at the first input collection, and is only guaranteed to * work if no number is present twice in this collection, in which case it * loops over the numbers contained in this collection (in no specific order) * and checks for each number whether it is also present in the second input * collection (which is also expected to have distinct numbers). The code * written in this loop adds the current number (from the first collection) * to the output collection if this number is also present in the second * collection, and does nothing with this number otherwise. */ Collection<Integer> filterNumbersThatAreShared(Collection<Integer> c1, Collection<Integer> c2); Solution A simpler signature and description could be:
/** * Returns the intersection of sets s1 and s2. */ Set<Integer> intersection(Set<Integer> s1, Set<Integer> s2); The following topics are beyond the scope of this course:
design patterns, Java frameworks, dependency injection, advanced functional typing, etc. Exercises and Quizzes # Most lectures will include exercises, and some will include an (anonymous) quizz. These are not part of your evaluation. We highly encourage you to try to solve them, by yourself. The purpose is to:
prepare you to the exam, make lectures more interactive, adapt the pace of the lectures based on your answers.

title: Pipeline
content:
Pipeline # Motivation # Syntax. Consider a chain of function applications, where the output of each function is the (unique) input to the next function in the chain. For instance
$\qquad h(g(f(x)))$
Computationally, this reads from right to left :
$\qquad$ “start with $x$, then apply $f$ to it, then apply $g$ to the result, and then $h$ to the result”.
Instead, when we write code, it can be convenient to use an expression that mimics the order of execution (when read from left to right).
For instance, we could define a reverse application symbol $\vartriangleright$ and write:
$\qquad ((x \vartriangleright f) \vartriangleright g) \vartriangleright h$
Or, assuming that $\vartriangleright$ associates to the left:
$\qquad x \vartriangleright f \vartriangleright g \vartriangleright h$
Note. A similar observation can be made for function composition: $g \circ f$ means “$f$ followed by $g$”. Instead, it can be more convenient to write $f$ before $g$.
Usage # In functional languages # Many functional languages provide operators for reverse application and/or composition, or let us define such operators (as higher-order functions).
These are often used to specify a sequence of operations (filter, transform, aggregate, etc.) applied to a stream of incoming data. This is analogous to a sequence of Unix-like pipes, where the output of a command is fed as input to the next command.
Thanks to lazy evaluation, each operation in the pipeline can start feeding partial results to the next one, even if it has not finished processing its input. This style of programming also pairs well with functors and monads, which we will see in later sections.
Beyond functional languages # A variety of imperative languages (notably C++, C#, Java or Rust) have borrowed or adapted this pattern. For instance, pipelines have become an essential part of Java programming, since the release of Java Stream API (2014).
This pattern has also made its way to scripting languages for statistical analysis, such as R (natively) or Matlab (via libraries).
in Haskell # Syntax. The Haskell module Data.Function provides a reverse application function called & (equivalent to the $\vartriangleright$ symbol that we used above). It is defined as expected:
(&) :: a -> (a -> b) -> b x & f = f x This function associates to the left, i.e. x & f & g means (x & f) & g
Example.
h (g (f x)) can be written
x & f & g & h Or more commonly:
x & f & g & h Exercise Recall the following functions:
even :: Int -> Bool, which maps an integer to True iff it is even, filter :: (a -> Bool) -> [a] -> [a], where filter f xs retains the elements of xs for which f evaluates to True, map :: (a -> b) -> [a] -> [b], where map f xs applies f to each elements of xs, all :: (a -> Bool) -> [a] -> Bool, where all f xs is True iff f evaluates to True for every element in xs maybeFirst :: [a] -> Maybe a, which maps a nonempty list its head (and the empty list to Nothing), maybeLast :: [a] -> Maybe a, which maps a nonempty list its last element (and the empty list to Nothing). Evaluate the following expressions.
[2,1,4,7] & filter even & map (+1) & maybeLast [-5,4,2,-1] & filter (\x -> x >= 0) & map (\x -> -x) & filter (\x -> x > -2) & all (not even) [ ("Bolzano", "BZ", 39100), ("Rovereto", "TN", 38068), ("Merano", "BZ", 39012), ("Bressanone", "BZ", 39042) ] & filter \(_, "BZ", zip) -> zip /= 39100 & map \(name, _, _) -> name & maybeFirst Solution Just 5 True Just "Merano" Exercise Simplify the following function.
myPipe :: [Int] -> Maybe Int myPipe xs = xs & filter even & map (+1) & filter even & maybeFirst Solution myPipe :: [Int] -> Maybe Int myPipe _ = Nothing Syntax.
We can use
forward function application: & from the module Data.Function reverse function composition: >>> form the module Control.Category Show how these can be used with filter, map, flatmap, foldr, etc.

title: Tuple
content:
Tuple # Syntax. A tuple in Haskell is written in parentheses.
Example. The pair $(\mathit{banana}, 5)$ is written
("banana", 5) Syntax. The type of a tuple is written in parentheses (like the tuple itself).
Examples.
Haskell tuple Possible type (5,3) (Int, Int) ("banana", 5) (String, Int) ("banana", 5, "tomato") (String, Int, String)

title: Conditions
content:
Conditions # Pattern matching # A pattern is a syntactic expression that can be matched against certain values.
Examples. Here are some basic patterns:
the pattern 2 matches the value 2, the pattern True matches the value True, a variable name (like x, y or xs) matches anything. Patterns let us define a function on a case-by-case basis.
Example. The function below maps 0 to 0, and every other integer to its successor. It uses the patterns 0 and x.
fancySucc:: Integer -> Integer fancySucc 0 = 0 fancySucc x = x + 1 Warning. The first pattern that matches a value takes precedence. So the order of patterns matters.
Exercise
What does the following function do? Can it be simplified?
dummySucc:: Integer -> Integer dummySucc x = x + 1 dummySucc 0 = 0 Solution.
It maps an integer to its successor. The second case is useless, because the input 0 is already handled by the first pattern. So the function is equivalent to
succ:: Integer -> Integer succ x = x + 1 Patterns can be composed into tuples
Example.
The following function maps a pairs of Boolean values to their conjunction.
and:: (Bool, Bool) -> Bool and (True,True) = True and (x,y) = False Exercise
Write the function above in curried form, and then in (curried) infix form:
Solution
Curried form.
and:: Bool -> Bool -> Bool and True True = True and x y = False Infix form.
(and):: Bool -> Bool -> Bool True and True = True x and y = False Exercise
What does the following function do?
(op):: Bool -> Bool -> Bool False op x = False True op x = x It is equivalent to the function and above.
Warning
A same variable name cannot be used twice on the left hand side of an equation.
Example.
The following is not valid syntactically in Haskell
(invalidXNor):: Bool -> Bool -> Bool x invalidXNor x = True x invalidXNor y = False The null pattern # The null pattern _ matches any value (like a variable name), but the value that it matches cannot be reused. It can be convenient to indicate that the value is irrelevant.
Example
The function (op) above can be written
(op):: Bool -> Bool -> Bool False op _ = False _ op x = x Contrary to a variable, the null pattern can be used several times on the left hand side of an equation. In such an equation, the values matched by _ can be different.
Example.
The function (and) above can be written
(and):: Bool -> Bool -> Bool True and True = True _ and _ = False Complex patterns # We will see later on that pattern matching extends to lists and composite types.
Boolean expression # Pattern matching has limitations. For instance, no pattern can match integers greater than 2, or odd integers.
For such conditions, we can use Boolean expressions
We have seen above how to define a Boolean operator. But this is not necessary: Haskell provides native implementations, which are similar their counterparts in Java (and most programming languages). They include:
conjunction, noted && disjunction, noted || negation, noted not Examples.
3 < 2 && 3 > 5 evaluates to False not (3 > 5 || True) evaluates to False Guards # A guard is a Boolean expression preceded with a |.
Similarly to patterns, guards let us define a function on a case-by-case basis. The first guard that evaluates to True determines the return value.
Example.
The following function maps an integer to its absolute value:
abs:: Integer -> Integer abs x | x >= 0 = x | x < 0 = -x There are two guard in this definition:
| x >= 0 | x < 0 Note.
The function above is equivalent to
abs:: Integer -> Integer abs x | x >= 0 = x | True = -x Haskell provides the keyword otherwise as an alias for True.
Example.
The function above can be written
abs:: Integer -> Integer abs x | x >= 0 = x | otherwise = -x Exercise.
Simplify the following function.
dummyComp:: Integer -> Integer -> Bool dummyComp x y | x <= y = True | x < y || x + 1 <= y = False | otherwise = True Solution.
dummyComp:: Integer -> Integer -> Bool dummyComp _ _ = True Syntax.
A guard can be used together with a pattern.
Example
fib :: Int -> Int fib 0 = 0 -- pattern only fib 1 = 1 -- pattern only fib n | n > 1 = fib (n-1) + fib (n-2) -- pattern + guard Syntax.
A pattern can also be used inside a guard, with a left arrow:
| <pattern> <- <expression> = <result> The evaluation goes as follows:
<expression> is evaluated first. Its value is matched against the pattern <pattern>. If the match succeeds, the guard is considered True and the right‑hand side (result) is selected. Otherwise control moves to the next next guard (if any). This can be helpful when <expression> is a call to another function.
Example.
TODO: fix this -- this function maps a character a pair that consists of a number and a Boolean analyzeChar :: Char -> (Int, Bool) analyzeChar c = (unicodeCodePoint c, c fib 0 = 0 -- pattern only fib 1 = 1 -- pattern only fib n | n > 1 = fib (n-1) + fib (n-2) -- pattern + guard Conditional statement # Haskell also supports “if then else” expressions, similar top their counterparts in Java (and many programming languages).
Example.
The following function maps:
a (strictly) positive integer to 1, 0 to 0 and a negative integer to -1. signum:: Integer -> Integer signum x = if x > 0 then 1 else if x == 0 then 0 else - 1 Warning. As opposed to Java, the else case is mandatory.
For instance, the following function definition is not syntactically valid.
invalid:: Integer -> Integer invalid _ = if True then 0 When there are more than 2 cases, guards can be easier to read than nested conditional statements.
Example.
Compare the function signum above to the same function written with guards:
signum:: Integer -> Integer signum x | x > 0 = 1 | x == 0 = 0 | otherwise = -1
