title: Control flow
content:
Control flow # Imperative languages # You should already be familiar with basic control flow in Java, in particular:
conditional statements (e.g. if (<...>) {<...>} else {<...>}) switch statements (for multiple cases), loops (while, for, etc.), method calls and return statements, etc. Most imperative languages (like C/C++, C#, JavaScript, Python, Rust, etc.) have similar constructions.
Functional programming # Conditions # Functional languages often provide conditional and switch-like statements that are similar to imperative ones.
In addition, a traditional feature of functional languages is pattern matching, which we will introduce in this chapter.
Interestingly, pattern matching is no longer exclusive to functional languages: a number of imperative languages now support it to some extent (either natively or via libraries). This includes C# (since 2020), Java (since 2021), PHP (since 2021), Python (since 2021) and Rust (since its first release in 2015).
Iteration # Pure functional programming does not admit loops. In particular, this is the case of Haskell.
So iteration in Haskell is primarily implemented via recursive functions.
However, Haskell also provides a convenient syntax for a limited form of iteration, called list comprehensions. These have also been integrated to Python since the release of Python 2 (in 2000).
Lazy evaluation # Functional programs tends to focus on what should be computed (rather than how), leaving more room to the compiler.
In particular, an important feature of functional languages (and Haskell in particular) is lazy evaluation, i.e.:
the evaluation of an expression can be cached and reused, computations can be delayed until they are strictly required. This is only possible if these computations have no side-effect.
Caching results (Point 1) # Example. Here is a simple illustration of Point 1 above (we will illustrate Point 2 in the chapter on recursion.
In the following Haskell program, if expensiveFunction is a pure function, then the expression expensiveFunction 2 is evaluated only once.
main :: IO () main = print $ expensiveFunction 2 && expensiveFunction 2 expensiveFunction :: Int -> Bool <...> Delaying computations (Point 2) # This form of lazy evaluation can be useful in a pipeline, e.g. to start processing an incoming stream of data before it has fully arrived (think of video streaming as an analogy).
This technique has made its way to many imperative languages, notably Java with the Stream API.
We will illustrate it in the chapter on recursion.
Content # In this chapter, we will cover:
pattern matching in Haskell and Java, other ways to check conditions in Haskell (guards and conditional statements), list comprehensions, in Haskell only (since they do not exist in Java), the [call stack] (language independent). Recursion will be studied in a dedicated chapter.

title: Function definition
content:
Function definition # Function # Syntax. A function in Haskell is defined analogously to a mathematical function, with the symbol =.
Example. The function $f(x) = 2x$ is written in Haskell
f x = 2 * x Syntax. Note that $f(x)$ is written f x, without parentheses. This also holds when applying a function to a constant.
Example. $f(15)$ is normally written
f 15 Function type # Syntax. The type of a Haskell function is specified immediately above its definition, as follows:
<functionName> :: <type> Example. The function $g\colon \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{Z}$ defined by $g(x) = x^2$ is written
g :: Integer -> Integer g x = x^2 Example. The following function maps even integers to True, and odd ones to False. It has type Integer -> Bool.
even :: Integer -> Bool even x = x % 2 == 0 In this definition, % is the so-called “modulo” operator: a % b is the remainder of the integer division of a by b.
Function types in Haskell are optional, but highly recommended.
Hint. Function types in Haskell can be automatically inferred.
So you may use them in two alternative ways:
For a simple function: implement the function, then use your IDE to generate a type for it. For a more complex function: write the function type, then implement the function, use your IDE to check that your implementation meets the type requirements. Note that the first option option may produce a type that is too generic for your needs.
Alias # Haskell provides several syntaxes to use aliases in function definitions.
<expression> where <alias> # Example. The two following functions are equivalent.
aFunction :: Int -> Bool aFunction x = (2 * x) % 3 == 1 || (x > 10 && (2 * x) % 3 == 2) theSameFunction :: Integer -> Bool theSameFunction x = x2Mod3 == 1 || (x > 10 && x2Mod3 == 2) where x2Mod3 = (2 * x) % 3 let <alias> in <expression> # Example. The following function is equivalent the ones above.
theSameFunctionAgain :: Integer -> Bool theSameFunctionAgain x = let x2Mod3 = (2 * x) % 3 in x2Mod3 == 1 || (x > 10 && x2Mod3 == 2) Warning. In some contexts, only one of the two syntaxes (where or let) can be used. See details here.
Composition # Syntax. The composition operator $\circ$ is written . in Haskell.
Example. The functions aFunction, theSameFunction and theSameFunctionAgain are equivalent in the program below (where the function unicodeCodePoint :: Char -> Int maps a character to its Unicode number).
even :: Int -> Bool even x = x % 2 == 0 aFunction :: Char -> Bool aFunction x = even (unicodeCodePoint x) theSameFunction :: Char -> Bool theSameFunction x = (even . unicodeCodePoint) x theSameFunctionAgain :: Char -> Bool theSameFunctionAgain = even . unicodeCodePoint Syntax. Function composition has precedence over function application. So in the example above, we can omit the parentheses:
theSameFunction :: Char -> Bool theSameFunction x = even . unicodeCodePoint x

title: Functor
content:
Functor # The notion of functor comes from a branch of mathematics called category theory.
For programmers, a functor can be viewed as a parameterized type that implements a certain function, while respecting certain constraints.
In Haskell # Notation. We will use t in this section to refer to a Haskell functor.
Definition. A functor in Haskell is a parameterized type t that implements a function
$\qquad$ fmap :: (a -> b) -> t a -> t b
In other words, fmap maps a function
$\qquad$ f :: a -> b
to a function
$\qquad$ fmap f :: t a -> t b
Example.
Maybe as a functor # Show how fmap g can be used in a pipeline with & or >>>, as long as g has type a -> b.
Spoiler: we will cover the case where g has type a -> Maybe b in the section on monads.

title: Monad
content:
Monad # Like functors, monads come from category theory.
For programmers, a monad can be viewed as a functor that implements additional functions, while respecting certain constraints.
In Haskell # We have seen in the previous section that a functor is a parameterized type that implements the function fmap.
A monad is a functor that implements two additional functions, called return and join.
Example. We have seen how the parameterized type “List” (written [ ] in Haskell) can be made a functor by implementing fmap.
We will see that it can also be made a monad, by implementing return and join.
Notation. We will use m in this section to refer to a Haskell monad.
Core functions # return # The function
return :: a -> m a specifies how to produce an element of type m a out of a single element of type a.
Example (continued). For the monad “List”, return wraps an element into a singleton list.
It can be defined as
return :: a -> [a] return x = [x] join # The function
join :: m (m a) -> m a “flattens” an m (m a) into an m a.
Example (continued). For the monad “List”, join concatenates the inner lists of a list of lists.
It can be defined as
join :: [[a]] -> [a] join xss = [ x | xs <- xss, x <- xs ] Concrete illustration.
data City = Bolzano | Merano | Trento | Rovereto deriving (Show) -- only needed for printing main :: IO () main = print $ join [[Bolzano, Merano], [Trento, Rovereto]] In this program, the expression
join [[Bolzano, Merano], [Trento, Rovereto]] flattens its input list, by concatenating the two inner lists.
So the program prints
[Bolzano, Merano, Trento, Rovereto] Derived functions # Two useful higher-order functions can be derived from join and fmap (recall that a monad is a functor, so it implements fmap). Both are infix functions, noted >>= and >=> respectivbely.
>>= (pronounced “bind” or “flatmap”) # The function >>= has type
(>>=) :: m a -> (a -> m b) -> m b Let x :: m a and f :: (a -> m b).
Then the evaluation of x >>= f:
applies f to each a “wrapped” inside x, and “flattens” (with join) the resulting m (m b) into an m b. Example (continued). For the monad “List”, the function >>= can be defined as follows:
(>>=) :: [a] -> (a -> [b]) -> [b] xs >>= f = [ y | x <- xs, y <- f x ] Concrete illustration (continued). Let us extend the program above with a type Province and a function cities, as follows:
data Province = AltoAdige | Trentino data City = Bolzano | Merano | Trento | Rovereto deriving (Show) -- only needed for printing cities :: Province -> [City] cities AltoAdige = [Bolzano, Merano] cities Trentino = [Trento, Rovereto] And modify the function main, as follows:
main :: IO () main = print $ [AltoAdige, Trentino] >>= cities The expression
[AltoAdige, Trentino] >>= cities applies the function cities to each of the two provinces, and flattens the resulting list.
So the program prints
[Bolzano, Merano, Trento, Rovereto] Observe that this is a flat list (rather than a list of lists).
Derivation. The function >>= need not be implemented if join and fmap already are. It can be derived as
(>>=) :: m a -> (a -> m b) -> m b x >>= f = join (fmap f x) Alternative definition of a monad. We can also implement >>= and derive join from it, as follows:
join :: m (m a) -> m a join x = x >>= id So there is an alternative (equivalent) way to define a monad, where >>= is primitive and join is derived from it.
You will find this alternative definition in some textbooks or websites.
Usage of >>= VS fmap VS function application # Recap. The following table recaps when >>=, fmap and plain function application can be used, for some function f and expression x (depending on their types).
For instance, the first line reads as follows:
$\qquad$ if x has type a and f has type a -> b, then f x has type b.
type of $x$ type of $f$ expression $e$ type of $e$ a a -> b f x b m a a -> b fmap f x m b m a a -> m b x >>= f m b Exercise Consider the following function:
preds :: Int -> [Int] preds x = [ y | y <- [0, 1 ..], y < x ] Complete the table below
expression is valid value (if valid) preds [3] no preds 3 yes [0,1,2] preds 0 3 >>= preds [3] >>= preds return 3 >>= preds [[3]] >>= preds [2,3] >>= preds fmap preds 3 fmap preds [2, 3] Solution expression is valid value (if valid) preds [3] no preds 3 yes [0,1,2] preds 0 yes [] 3 >>= preds no [3] >>= preds yes [0,1,2] return 3 >>= preds yes [0, 1, 2] [[3]] >>= preds no [2,3] >>= preds yes [0, 1, 0, 1, 2] fmap preds 3 no fmap preds [2, 3] yes [[0, 1], [0, 1, 2]] >=> (pronounced “fish”) # Derivation. The function >=> is best explained by its derivation from >>=:
(>=>) :: (a -> m b) -> (b -> m c) -> (a -> m c) (f >=> g) x = f x >>= g Syntax. the parentheses in (f >>= g) x are unnecessary. We only added them for clarity.
Intuition. The function >=> “chains” two functions
f :: a -> m b g :: b -> m c into a function
f >=> g :: a -> m c This function f >=> g applies (>>= g) to the output of f.
Example (continued). For the monad “List”, the function >=> can equivalently be defined as:
(>=>) :: (a -> [b]) -> (b -> [c]) -> a -> [c] (f >=> g) x = [ y | xs <- f x, y <- g xs ] Concrete illustration (continued). Let us extend the program above with a type Region and a function provinces, as follows:
data Region = TrentinoAltoAdige data Province = AltoAdige | Trentino data City = Bolzano | Merano | Trento | Rovereto deriving (Show) -- only needed for printing provinces :: Region -> [Province] provinces TrentinoAltoAdige = [AltoAdige, Trentino] cities :: Province -> [City] cities AltoAdige = [Bolzano, Merano] cities Trentino = [Trento, Rovereto] And modify the function main, as follows:
main :: IO () main = print $ (provinces >=> cities) TrentinoAltoAdige The expression
(provinces >=> cities) TrentinoAltoAdige is equivalent to
provinces TrentinoAltoAdige >>= cities So the program still prints
[Bolzano, Merano, Trento, Rovereto] Note. The relation between >>= and >=> is analogous to the relation between function application and function composition (or between their reversed versions & and >>>).
Usage. In some places, >=> can be more convenient than >>=, in the same way that function composition (e.g. g . f) can be more convenient than function application (e.g. g (f x)).
Concrete illustration (continued). The function definition
extractCities :: Region -> [Cities] extractCities r = provinces r >>= cities can be simplified as
extractCities :: Region -> [Cities] extractCities = provinces >=> cities Constraints # We have seen previously that the function fmap must satisfy additional constraints for a parameterized type to be a valid functor.
Similarly, the functions implemented by a monad must satisfy certain constraints.
One can formulate these constraints by focusing on the >=> and return functions, as follows:
Constraints. A monad must satisfy two constraints:
>=> is associative, i.e. $\qquad$ (f >=> g) >=> h = f >=> (g >=> h)
return is a neutral element for >=>, i.e. $\qquad$ f >=> return = return >=> f = f
Alternative formulations. There are several common (equivalent) ways to formulate the constraints that a monad must satisfy. In particular, they can be expressed as constraints over join or >>=, rather than >=> (as we did here).
You may find these alternative definition in some textbooks or websites.
Maybe as a monad # We have seen above how the functor “List” (noted [ ] in Haskell) can be made a monad, by implementing the functions return an join.
We will now do this with the functor Maybe.
As a reminder, Maybe is declared in the Haskell Prelude as:
data Maybe a = Nothing | Just a Core functions # return :: a -> m a # The function return for the type Maybe simply wraps a value (with the constructor Just):
return :: a -> Maybe a return x = Just x join :: m (m a) -> m a # The function join for the type Maybe transforms a Maybe (Maybe a) into a Maybe a in the expected way:
join :: Maybe (Maybe a) -> Maybe a join Just (Just x) = Just x -- covers the two remaining cases: `Nothing` and `Just Nothing` join _ = Nothing Derived functions # Bind, a.k.a. >>= :: m a -> (a -> m b) -> m b # The function >>= for the type Maybe can be defined as
(>>=) :: (Maybe a) -> (a -> Maybe b) -> Maybe b -- propagate `Nothing` Nothing >>= _ = Nothing -- otherwise apply the function Just x >>= f = f x Equivalently, it can be derived from join and fmap, as explained above.
The function >>= is convenient to chain computations that may “fail” (i.e. return Nothing): it lets us write code that automatically takes care of propagating failures (a.k.a. “null values”).
Example. Consider the two following functions
safeDiv :: Int -> Int -> Maybe Int safeDiv _ 0 = Nothing safeDiv x y = Just (x / y) maybeLog :: Int -> Maybe Float maybeLog x | x > 0 = Just (logBase 2 x) | otherwise = Nothing Intuitively,
safeDiv only “succeeds” for a divisor $\neq 0$, and maybeLog only “succeeds” for an input $> 0$. The function >>= lets us feed the output of safeDiv to maybeLog, without the need to check whether safeDiv succeeded.
For instance:
safeDiv 24 3 >>= maybeLog evaluates to 3 (because safeDiv and maybeLog both “succeed”) safeDiv (-24) 3 >>= maybeLog evaluates to Nothing (because maybeLog “fails”) safeDiv 4 0 >>= maybeLog also evaluates to Nothing (because safeDiv “fails”, and >>= propagates the failure). Exercise Recall the following functions:
even :: Int -> Bool, which maps an integer to True iff it is even, filter :: (a -> Bool) -> [a] -> [a], where filter f xs retains the elements of xs for which f evaluates to True, maybeLast :: [a] -> Maybe a, which maps a nonempty list its last element (and the empty list to Nothing), maybeLog :: Int -> Maybe Float from our the example immediately above. Now consider the following pipeline function:
myPipe :: [Int] -> Maybe Float myPipe xs = xs & filter even & maybeLast >>= maybeLog Evaluate the following expressions
myPipe [] myPipe [8] myPipe [3,5] myPipe [4,3,-2,5] Solution myPipe :: [Int] -> Maybe Float myPipe xs = xs & filter even & maybeLast >>= maybeLog myPipe [] evaluates to Nothing (maybeLast “fails” and >>= propagates Nothing) myPipe [8] evaluates to Just 3 (maybeLast and maybeLog both “succeed”) myPipe [3,5] evaluates to Nothing (maybeLast “fails” and >>= propagates Nothing) myPipe [4,3,-2,5] evaluates to Nothing (maybeLast “succeeds” and maybeLog “fails”) Fish, a.k.a >=> :: (a -> m b) -> (b -> m c) -> a -> m c # Example. Using >=> and forward function composition (>>>), we can rewrite the pipeline of the exercise immediately above in a more concise fashion:
myPipe :: [Int] -> Maybe Float myPipe = filter even >>> maybeLast >=> maybeLog To go further # [!TODO] Mention the “procedural”-like do notation, which is syntactic sugar for a sequences of binds + return.
[!TODO] Mention the State Monad, and more generally the possibility to perform computations with side effects in a controlled way, i.e.:
the impure part is taken care of by the higher-level functions (>>=, >=>, return, etc.) the rest of the code consists of pure functions [!TODO] For a good tutorial, refer to this page

title: Parameterized type
content:
Parameterized type # In Haskell # Syntax # We have seen how Haskell uses constructors to define composite types.
Example (reminder). As we have seen earlier, the type of lists of integers can be defined as follows:
data IntList = Nil | Cons Int (IntList) Similarly, the type of lists of Booleans can be defined as
data BoolList = Nil | Cons Bool (BoolList) If we want to make this more generic, we can use a type variable instead of the concrete type (Int or Bool here).
Syntax. A parameterized type declaration in Haskell has the form
data <typeName> <first type variable> ... <last typeVariable> = <definition> where <definition> is a type definition that may use the type variables declared on the left-hand side.
Example (continued). The parameterized type “list of a’s” (where a is a type variable) can be defined analogously to the two types above, as
data List a = Nil | Cons a (List a) This type is equivalent to the native Haskell type [a] that we have used in the previous section.
Example. Recall the type IntBT of binary trees with integer values that we used earlier:
data IntBT = Empty | Node Int IntBT IntBT We can also declare a parameterized version BT aof this type, where nodes carry a value of type a:
data BT a = Empty | Node a (BT a) (BT a) Generic functions # Arbitrary type parameter # As we have seen already, the parameterized type [a] lets us write functions for arbitrary lists (regardless of the specific type of elements in a list).
Example (reminder)
length :: [a] -> Int length [] = 0 length (_ : xs) = 1 + length xs Similarly, we can modify some of the functions that we wrote for a tree of integers, so that they work for arbitrary tree.
Example. Recall the function height :: IntBT -> Int that we have seen in the section on binary recursion.
data IntBT = Empty | Node Int IntBT IntBT height :: IntBT -> Int height Empty = 0 height Node i t1 t2 = 1 + max (height t1) (height t2) The same implementation would work for a tree of strings, a tree of Boolean values, etc.
So we can use our parameterized type BT a to write a generic version of our function:
data BT a = Empty | Node a (BT a) (BT a) height :: BT _ -> Int -- Same implementation as the function `height :: IntBT -> Int above` <...> Observe that the implementation is identical (only the type of the function is affected).
Note. In this example, we could equivalently have used the declaration
height :: BT a -> Int We used the wildcard type _ instead of a for clarity, because the value of a in this example is irrelevant.
Bounded type parameter # Example (reminder). Recall the function contains :: Int -> IntBT -> Bool that we have seen in the section
data IntBT = Empty | Node Int IntBT IntBT contains :: Int -> IntBT -> Bool contains Empty = False contains i Node j t1 t2 = i == j || contains i t1 || contains i t2 As seen int the previous section, we cannot replace Int with any type here, because the function makes use of equality. So we can only generify this function to types that support equality, as follows:
data BT a = Empty | Node a (BT a) (BT a) contains :: Eq a => a -> BT a -> Bool Again, the implementation of the function is unchanged (only its type is affected).

title: Additional resources
content:
Additional resources # You are free to use additional resources (web sites, IDE generated code, LLMs, etc.) to solve the course’s assignments.
However, make sure that you understand the code that you submit.
Java and Haskell syntax # This website contains code snippets that illustrate how to perform certain operations. However, this documentation is far from exhaustive (on purpose).
When it comes to syntax, the first two sources of complementary material for this course are:
the documentation of the libraries (standard and auxiliary) used in assignments. An IDE is often the fastest way to navigate through it, and the web. Books # An (optional) reading list for this course is available in the catalogue of the Unibz’s library. All books can be accessed via the library (either physically or digitally).
Among these books, we highlight the following five:
Introductory textbooks # Java # Java : the complete reference (11th edition) by Herbert Schildt McGraw-Hill Education, 2019
This is a general introductory book to Java and object oriented programming. It covers most topics seen in this course (and a lot more).
Head First Java (3rd edition) by Kathy Sierra, Bert Bates and Trisha Gee O’Reilly Media, 2022
This is an alternative introductory book, less comprehensive, but with more emphasis on problem solving (rather than syntax).
Haskell # Lear you a Haskell for great good by Miran Lipovača No Starch Press, 2012
A short introduction to Haskell’s syntax and basic usage.
Programming in Haskell (2nd edition) by Graham Hutton Cambridge University Press, 2016
A more complete introduction to Haskell
Good practices (intermediate level) # Effective Java (3rd edition)
by Joshua Bloch
Addison-Wesley Professional, 2017
This is a reference book for best practices and common pitfalls when programming in Java (and more generally in an object-oriented language).

title: Program
content:
Program # File # A simple Haskell program is written in a file with the extension .hs. For instance, MyProgram.hs.
Content # Functions # A Haskell program primarily consists of function definitions.
Hint. The order of function definitions in a program is irrelevant.
Main function # To make a program executable, one of its functions must be the entry point. By default, this function is called main, and has type IO () (more on this type later).
Example. This is a small executable Haskell program:
square :: Integer -> Integer square x = x^2 main :: IO () main = print $ square 3 Syntax. The $ operator is an alternative syntax for function application. In this example, this is just a replacement for parentheses. So the last line is equivalent to:
main = print (square 3) Reminder. Types in Haskell are optional, but highly recommended. They can be generated by your IDE.
Modules # A Haskell program can import other Haskell programs, called modules.
Importing a module # Every program imports a default module called the Haskell Prelude. It contains most of the functions and types that we will need in this course.
If you want to import another module, add the following to your file, before any function definition:
import <moduleName> Example. The following program imports the module Data.Map.
import Data.Map <...> main :: IO () main = <...> Warning. If you define a function that is already defined in the Prelude (or in an imported module), then your program will not compile.
Example. The function reverse is already defined in the Prelude. So if we try to implement it, we should give a different name, for instance reverse'.
Declaring a module # Optionally, you can make your own program a module (which lets you import it in other programs).
Syntax. A module name must start with a capital letter.
Syntax. To make your program a module, start with (assuming that you want to name your module MyModule):
module MyModule where Syntax. By convention, the name of a module is often the name of its file, without extension.
Comments # A commented line starts with --. A commented block is between {- and -}. Example.
-- Squares an integer square :: Integer -> Integer square x = x^2 {- Computes the factorial of a non‑negative integer. Does not terminate for a negative integer. -} factorial :: Integer -> Integer -- Base case factorial 0 = 1 -- Inductive case factorial n = n * factorial (n - 1) Compilation, execution # Your IDE should provide a way to compile and run an executable program. Otherwise, within a terminal (assuming that your file is named MyProgram.hs), run:
runghc MyProgram.hs

title: Programming paradigms
content:
Programming paradigms # A programming paradigm is a way to write programs. Programming languages can be categorized based on the paradigms (and sub-paradigms) that they support:
imperative programming, object-oriented programming, functional programming, logical programming, constraint programming, etc. Warning. The boundaries of programming paradigms are not always clear, and can be subjective. For this reason, we will (cautiously) stick to Wikipedia content when describing them.
Imperative programming # The imperative (sometimes called procedural) paradigm is the most widely used. Imperative languages include (in alphabetical order):
C/C++, C#, Go, Java, Javascript, Kotlin, Lua, Perl, PHP, Python, Rust, Ruby, Scala, Typescript, Visual Basic, etc. From Wikipedia:
“Imperative programming focuses on describing how a program operates step by step […] The term is often used in contrast to declarative programming, which focuses on what the program should accomplish without specifying all the details of how the program should achieve the result.”
Warning. The imperative VS declarative (a.k.a. “how” VS “what”) distinction is not binary, but lies on a spectrum: some languages or programs are more imperative/declarative than others.
Object-oriented programming (OOP) # Object-oriented languages are (usually) also imperative. These include:
C++, C#, Java/Kotlin/Scala, Ruby, Visual Basic, etc. However, most (modern) imperative language offer some OOP features: Rust, Javascript/Typescript, Python, etc.
From Wikipedia:
“An OOP computer program consists of objects that interact with one another.”
OOP allows structuring code in a modular and readable way, while avoiding code duplication. OOP is widely used in moderate to large sized projects.
Java # Java is one of the most popular object-oriented languages.
Java’s syntax is influenced by C++ (albeit without pointers and explicit memory allocation), and C# was in turn heavily influenced by Java.
Java (like C++ and C#) has been extended with functional features over the years, notably with the release of Java 8 (2014).
In this course, we will use Java 1.21 (more commonly called Java 21).
Functional programming # Functional languages are less common than imperative ones. They include:
Erlang, Haskell, Lisp dialects (Common Lisp, F#, OCaml, etc.) ML dialects (Clojure, Scheme, Standard ML, etc.) Scala, etc. However, some functional features are key to popular imperative languages:
callback methods in Javascript, list comprehensions in Python, predefined higher-order functions (map, filter, reduce, etc.) for data processing in Rust and modern C++, C# and Java, immutability, etc. From Wikipedia:
“Functional programming has its roots in academia, evolving from the lambda calculus, a formal system of computation based only on functions.”
The functional paradigm, “declares the desired result as the value of a series of function evaluations”, and “avoids state and mutable data.”
“Proponents of purely functional programming claim that by restricting side effects, programs can have fewer bugs, be easier to debug and test, and be more suited to formal verification.”

title: Type parameter
content:
Type parameter # A type can have one or several types as parameter(s), represented as type variable(s).
Example. “List of integers” and “List of strings” are two similar yet distinct types.
The parameterized type “List of $X$” (where $X$ is a type variable) captures this similarity.
Terminology. In object oriented programming, a parameterized type is often called a generic type or a generic. In particular, this is the case in Java.
Content # In this chapter, we will see:
types variables in Haskell and Java, basic parameterized types in Haskell and Java, covariant and contravariant types (in Java only), i.e. how parameterized types interact with subtyping.

title: Composite type
content:
Composite type # Some programming languages let us define our own types inductively, by combining values and/or types.
We will see how this is done in Haskell and Java.
Terminology. Composite types are sometimes called algebraic types or algebraic data types (ADTs), because they are build via operations over types that are analogous to algebraic operations (notably sum and product) .

title: List
content:
List # Recursive form # Lists in Haskell are recursive.
Definition. In Haskell:
[] is the empty list, if xs is a list and x an element, then x:xs is a list. In addition, all elements in a list must have the same type. If this type is a, then the list has type [a].
Example.
The Haskell list 5 : (2 : []) stands for the mathematical list $5,2$. [Int] is a valid type for this list (as well as [Integer] or [Float]), because Int is a valid type for 5 and 2 .
Warning. The empty list [] has type [a] for any type a.
Examples.
The following types are valid for the empty list (among other):
[Int] (list of bounded integers) [Integer] (list of arbitrary integers) [String] (list of strings) [[String]] (list of lists of strings) etc. The operator : (pronounced “cons”) associates to the right, so parentheses are often redundant.
Examples.
list in Haskell omitting parentheses $2$ 2 : [] 2 : [] $5, 2$ 5 : (2 : []) 5 : 2 : [] $3, 5, 2$ 3 : (5 : (2 : [])) 3 : 5 : 2 : [] $\textit{b,d,a}$ 'b' : ('d' : ('a' : [])) 'b' : 'd' : 'a' : [] Terminology. If x:xs is a list, then:
x is called its head, xs is called its tail. The head is intuitively the first element of the list, and the tail is the rest of it.
Note. If the list x : xs has type [a], then
x has type a, and xs has type [a]. Syntax. As syntactic sugar, a list can also be written in non-recursive form, within square brackets.
Examples.
Haskell list brackets notation 2 : [] [2] 5 : 2 : [] [5,2] 3 : 5 : 2 : [] [3,5,2] 'b' : 'd' : 'a' : [] ['b','d','a'] Concatenation # Syntax. The operator ++ concatenates two lists.
Examples.
expression evaluation [2,1] ++ [3,2,5] [2,1,3,2,5] [2,1] ++ [] [2,1] [] ++ [3,2,5] [3,2,5] Exercise.
Evaluate the foilwing expressions (mix of :, ++ and nested lists).
List patterns # [] is a pattern. It matches the empty list. If p and q are patterns, then (p:q) is a pattern. It matches a list whose head is matched by p, and whose tail is matched by q. If p is a pattern, then [p] is a pattern, equivalent to (p:[]). If p and q are patterns, then [p,q] is a pattern, equivalent to (p:q:[]). etc. Example. The following function discards the first element of a list, unless the list has length $\le 1$.
discardHead :: [Int] -> [Int] discardHead [] = [] discardHead [x] = [x] discardHead (_:xs) = xs Exrecise
Write a function startsWithTwins that maps a list of pairs of strings to:
True if its head contains twice the same string, and False otherwise Possible solution
startsWithTwins :: [(String,String)] -> Bool startsWithTwins [] = False startsWithTwins ((x,y) : _) = x == y List comprehension # We have seen [earlier] the (mathematical) syntax for set comprehensions.
Example (reminder). If $X$ is a set of numbers, then the expression
$\qquad\{x^2 \mid x \in X, x \ge 5\}$
means “all $x^2$ such that $x \in X$ and $x \ge 5$”.
This is the set that consists of all numbers greater or equal to $5$ in $X$, each squared.
Haskell provides a similar syntax for lists (Python as well, since Python 2 in 2000).
Example. In Haskell, if xs is a list of numbers, then we can write
$\qquad$ [x^2 | x <- xs, x >= 5]
This expression evaluates to the list of all x^2 such that x is in xs and x >= 5.
Terminology. In the example above, the expression x <- xs is called a generator.
A list comprehension may have several generators.
Example. Here is a list comprehension with two generators:
$\qquad$ [x + y | x <- xs, y <- ys]
It evaluates to the list of all x + y such that x is in xs and y is in ys.
Warning. For a list (as opposed to set) comprehension:
the order of the output list reflects the order of the initial list(s), the output list may contain duplicates. Examples.
[x^2 | x <- [3,5,2]] evaluates to [9,25,4] [x^2 | x <- [-3,3]] evaluates to [9,9] [x^2 | x <- [3,5,2], x < 5] evaluates to [9,4] [x + y | x <- [3,5,2], y <- [2,3]] evaluates to [5,6,7,8,4,5] Exercise
The function sum maps a list of numbers to their sum. For instance, sum [2,3,6] evaluates to 11.
What does the following function do?
myFunction:: [Int] -> Int myFunction xs = sum [1 | x <- xs] Solution
It returns the length of the input list.
For instance
Exercise
What does the following function do?
myFunction:: [[Int]] -> [Int] myFunction xss = [x | xs <- xss, x <- xs ] Solution
It concatenates all lists in an input list of lists.
For instance
myFunction [[1,3], [2,5], [1,3,4]] evaluates to [1,3,2,5,1,3,4]
Exercise
Write a function firstsIfSecPos that maps a list of pairs of integers to a list that consists of the first element of each pair, if the second element is $\ge 0$.
For instance firstsIfSecPos [(3,4), (5,-2), (-3,6), (1,3)] evaluates to [3, -3, 1]
Solution
firstsIfSecPos:: [(Int, Int)] -> [Int] firstsIfSecPos ps = [x | (x,y) <- ps, y >= 0]

content: Functional patterns # This chapter gathers a few high-level patterns commonly used in functional programs. We will illustrate most of them with Haskell, but also see how some of of them have made their way to Java, notably via the Stream interface.
